chunk_summary: Guidelines for minimum exclusion work-up in children with persistent ALT elevation to rule out treatable mimics before diagnosing MASLD, emphasizing targeted testing and early referral for high-risk cases.
learning_objectives: Understand the minimum exclusion work-up for persistent ALT elevation in pediatric MASLD screening.
Identify key triggers for escalating evaluation and early referral in suspected MASLD cases.
Differentiate between simple fatty liver and higher-risk liver diseases in children.
key_facts: MASLD is a diagnosis of exclusion, requiring the elimination of treatable mimics such as viral hepatitis, thyroid dysfunction, celiac disease, Wilson disease, and autoimmune hepatitis.
Minimum exclusion tests include HBsAg, anti-HCV, TSH, free T4, IgA tissue transglutaminase, serum ceruloplasmin, ANA, ASMA, and total IgG.
Persistent ALT >2× sex-specific upper limit of normal for >3 months or ALT >80 U/L are triggers for escalation.
Additional red flags include hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, falling platelets, jaundice, or impaired synthetic liver function.
Drug-induced liver injury from medications like anti-TB drugs, antiepileptics, or steroids must be ruled out.
Early referral to pediatric hepatology is recommended for syndromic features or suspected inborn errors of metabolism.
reference_quote: MASLD is a diagnosis of exclusion. Before labeling a child with MASLD, rule out common and treatable mimics that are clinically relevant in India and can coexist with obesity. (ISPGHAN/Indian Pediatrics, 2024)
document_key_topics: Minimum exclusion work-up for persistent ALT elevation
Triggers for escalation and referral in pediatric MASLD
Differentiating simple fatty liver from higher-risk diseases
